examples.title: Basic Function Definition and Implicit Type Casting
examples.description: Demonstrates basic function definition, parameter passing, and implicit type casting when passing arguments to functions.
examples.explanation:
1. The function calculateArea() expects a double parameter but receives an int (5), which is implicitly cast to double.
2. The function getIntegerPart() returns an int but the calculation involves double, which is implicitly cast to int (truncates decimal).
3. In C++, implicit type casting (promotion/demotion) happens automatically when there's no data loss or when converting to a 'wider' type.

examples.title: Explicit Type Casting with static_cast and Function Overloading
examples.description: Demonstrates explicit type casting using static_cast and function overloading for different data types.
examples.explanation:
1. Function overloading allows multiple functions with the same name but different parameter types.
2. static_cast<> is the C++ way for explicit type conversions - it's checked at compile time.
3. C-style casts (type)value are less safe and harder to find in code.
4. const_cast is used to add or remove const qualifier.
5. The function selects the appropriate overload based on argument types at compile time.

examples.title: Template Functions with Type Casting and Dynamic Polymorphism
examples.description: Advanced example showing function templates with type casting, inline functions, and reference parameters.
examples.explanation:
1. Template functions allow generic programming - the same function works with different data types.
2. The -> decltype(auto) syntax allows return type deduction based on the expression.
3. Inline functions suggest the compiler to replace function calls with the function body for performance.
4. Reference parameters (int&) allow direct modification of the original variable.
5. const int& allows reading without copying while preventing modification.
6. Conversion operators (operator int(), operator double()) enable implicit and explicit conversions from user-defined types to primitive types.
